feedback.text: To the European Commission, Im writing to share some thoughts on the Digital Fairness Act, particularly on an issue thats becoming increasingly urgent for millions of consumers: the ability of publishers to unilaterally disable access to video games that people have purchased fairly and in good faith. This concern is at the heart of the Stop Killing Games initiative, which has already gathered over 1 million signatures from players around the world. The message is clearconsumers are tired of paying for games that can later be taken away with little to no warning, often leaving them with nothing to show for their purchase. Unfortunately, this is not just a hypothetical concern. Games like The Crew (Ubisoft), Battleborn (2K), Mirrors Edge Catalyst (EA), and DuckTales Remastered (Capcom) have all been delisted, disabled, or rendered unplayable due to server shutdowns or DRM deactivation. In some cases, even the single-player components stop working. People who paid for these titles now have no way to access or preserve them. Even more worrying is the fact that, as this movement gains momentum, lobbyist groups from major publishers are already organizing to push backtrying to influence regulation in a direction that protects their profits, not the rights of consumers. Theres a real danger that without strong, clear protections in place, this kind of corporate behavior will become the norm across the entire industry. We urge the European Commission to recognize this moment and act decisively. The Digital Fairness Act is an opportunity to set a global precedent: that digital purchases must come with real, enforceable rights. Consumers should not be left helpless when companies decide a product they bought should simply stop working. We hope you will stand with the millions calling for fairness, transparency, and accountability in the digital entertainment space. Thank you for your continued work on behalf of digital consumers. Sincerely, A concerned gamer and supporter of the Stop Killing Games initiative
